blocks.text: Sandra Flores
Chief Operating Officer
Poole, Mcknight and Rush
Re: Capital Allocation Review
Dear Ms. Flores:

This letter confirms our engagement to review how {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}} allocates its capital, and it sets out the terms on which we will carry the work out. It records what we will examine, who will examine it, the fee, and the conditions both of us work under. I have written it to be read against your own memory of our conversations. If any term here differs from what you took away from them, telephone me before you countersign and we will put it right at once. An engagement of this kind holds together only when both sides share one understanding of scope from the first day, and this letter is where we fix that understanding.
What We Will Examine

The review covers how capital is allocated across your business, and within that it does four things. We will document the process as it stands today: how a capital request is raised, how it is evaluated, who approves it, how it is funded, and whether anyone returns to it once the money is spent. We will build a picture of where capital has actually gone over recent years, sorted by purpose, growth, maintenance, and compliance, and by the unit that spent it. We will test the criteria used to weigh one request against another, the hurdle rates, the payback thresholds, and the judgments of strategic fit, and we will see whether those criteria are applied the same way in every part of the business. And we will recommend a method for ranking capital requests and a governance process for the decisions behind them, in a form your management can operate without us.
Outside This Review

Some matters sit outside this work, and I will name them so our expectations are on the record from the start. This is not an audit or any form of attestation, and it yields no opinion on your financial statements. We do not value the business or any part of it. We give no legal or tax advice; where a question turns on either, we will flag it in writing and refer you to your own advisers. And we do not choose which projects you fund. We will hand you a defensible way to rank them and the ranking our method produces, and the decision on any individual project remains yours.
How We Will Work

We work from findings outward, and we document as we go. Every figure that reaches a deliverable will trace back to a workpaper we can produce on request. Where a conclusion rests on a judgment rather than a number, we will say so and show the judgment. We will bring you interim results as they firm up, so that nothing in the final material reads as a surprise. And we keep findings apart from recommendations, and label each for what it is, because the two carry different weight and should not be read as one.
Who Will Do the Work

Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager, runs the engagement day to day and is your point of contact on anything of substance. Brenda Clayton, Consultant, leads the analysis of your capital process and the operating-model questions beneath it. I read every deliverable before it leaves this office. For scheduling, for data requests, and for any matter that needs a decision on your side, we will work through you, Sandra Flores, unless you point us to someone else.
Fee and Start

Our fee to {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}} for the review is {{fact:f:E-2019-003.fee}}, fixed for the scope set out above. We will begin on {{fact:f:E-2019-003.start}}, and we plan on roughly six months from that point to the final deliverable. If you ask us to widen the scope, we will quote the addition in writing and will not start it until you have agreed the price.
Invoicing

We bill the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each payable within thirty days of the invoice date. Out-of-pocket costs, principally travel to your offices, are billed at cost and itemized on the invoice. We do not mark expenses up.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from the start date above until we deliver the final material, unless one of us ends it sooner. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice, with or without reason. On termination you owe the fee for work performed to that date, prorated against the fixed fee, together with expenses incurred, and we will turn over our work product in whatever state it has reached.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you give us in confidence, use it only for this engagement, and disclose it outside the firm only as you direct in writing or as law and professional standards require. Your capital plans are commercially sensitive, and we will treat them accordingly. We ask the same of you as to our methods, our workpapers, and the terms of this fee.
Limitation of Liability

Our total liability arising from this engagement, whether in contract, in tort, or on any other footing, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it. Neither of us will be liable to the other for indirect, incidental, or consequential loss, lost profits among them, however it arises. That limit does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use; if you mean to put one before a third party who may rely on it, raise it with me first so we can address the reliance in writing.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Any dispute we cannot settle between us goes to mediation before either party takes it to court.

If these terms are acceptable, please countersign both copies and return one to us; the second is yours to keep. We are glad to take on this work for Poole, Mcknight and Rush, and we will be ready to start on the date set out above.
Sincerely,
To: Deborah Jimenez
From: Brenda Clayton
Subject: Kickoff, Capital Allocation Review for Poole, Mcknight and Rush

This engagement begins on {{fact:f:E-2019-003.start}}. What we owe {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}} is a clear account of how it allocates capital today and a method it can use to allocate it better, set down in a form the client can run without us standing over it. In concrete terms that is four deliverables: a map of the current capital process, a history of where the capital has gone, an assessment of the criteria the client uses to weigh one request against another, and a recommended way to rank requests and govern the decisions behind them. The engagement letter is signed, so we are working to that scope and not reopening it. I have set this memo out in the order I expect us to work, and each part below names who carries it.
The work, in four parts

items: The current process. I will document how a capital request is raised, how it is evaluated, who signs off, how it is funded, and whether anyone returns to it after the money is spent. We start here, because every later piece is read against it.

The spending history. We build a picture of where capital has gone over recent years, split by purpose and by the unit that spent it, drawn from the client's own records rather than from anyone's memory of them.

The evaluation criteria. We test the hurdle rates, the payback thresholds, and the strategic judgments the client applies, and we check whether they hold steady across the business or drift from one unit to the next. My working assumption is that they drift, but the point is to show it, not to assert it.

The recommendation. We design a single method for ranking requests and a governance process for the decisions. This piece follows the first three and does not run ahead of them; a ranking method built before we understand the current one would be a guess.

blocks.text: Open questions for the team

items: Which units are in scope. The letter frames the review across the whole business; before we map anything I want a written list of the units we are actually covering, so we do not spend a week documenting a division no one asked about.

Who owns the numbers on the client side. Deborah Jimenez will settle a single point of contact with Sandra Flores, the client's Chief Operating Officer, so our questions do not scatter across their finance staff and come back inconsistent.
How far back the history runs. We agree the number of years before we pull the data, not after, so we are not re-pulling it a month from now.

blocks.text: Immediate steps

items: I circulate one consolidated data request this week and open a log of what we have asked for and what has come back.
Deborah Jimenez confirms data access and the client point of contact with Sandra Flores.
We hold our first internal working session at the end of next week. Bring what you have, including the gaps you have not yet closed.

blocks.text: We send the client nothing that characterizes a result at this stage, because there is no result to characterize. We have a scope, a team, and a data request going out this week. I will keep the request log current, so that at any point the three of us know exactly what is still outstanding and who we are waiting on.

To: Sandra Flores
Cc: Deborah Jimenez
Sandra,

Quick update on where we are with the capital review. The consolidated data request has gone in and most of it is back. I have the current-state process largely mapped from what your finance group sent over, and the spend history for the last several years is coming together. Two items are still open on our side, and neither is a concern yet.

To close them I need two things from Poole, Mcknight and Rush. The first is the approval file for the two larger projects from last year, so we can see how a request of that size actually moves through the process rather than how the policy says it should. The second is a name: whoever owns the capital request log, so I am not routing the same question to three different people. If you can point me to that person, a short call would sort it out.

One scheduling note. We are still on for the working session; I will send Deborah Jimenez the agenda beforehand so your team knows what we will cover. Nothing we bring will be a finding yet. The record for {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}} is coming together, but it is a picture of the current state, not a conclusion about it.
Thanks,
Brenda Clayton
Working session on the Capital Allocation Review for {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}}. Minutes recorded by Deborah Jimenez.
Present

items: Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager
Brenda Clayton, Consultant
Sandra Flores, Chief Operating Officer, Poole, Mcknight and Rush

blocks.text: Discussion

The session reviewed the current-state work. Brenda Clayton presented the process map: how a capital request is raised, evaluated, approved, and funded within the business. The map is largely complete. One step is thinly documented, the post-investment review, because it appears to happen rarely and without a standard record. Where the practice is inconsistent the map shows the range rather than a single path. Brenda Clayton walked the group through two units whose steps differ enough that a request of the same size follows a noticeably different path in each.

The criteria used to weigh requests are not applied uniformly. Some units test a request against a hurdle rate; others do not. The spending threshold that sends a request to committee differs from one unit to the next. This is recorded as an observation. It is not yet a recommendation, and nothing in it should be read as one at this stage. Brenda Clayton noted that the divergence itself is the useful finding, since it explains why comparable requests fare differently depending on where they originate.

Deborah Jimenez confirmed the units in scope with Sandra Flores, so the review covers the parts of the business the engagement letter named and no others. The team also agreed that data requests to the client will route through a single point of contact. Sending the same question to several people had already produced two answers that did not match, and a single route avoids that.

The spending history is assembled for recent years and split by purpose and by unit. One unit's records are incomplete. Sandra Flores agreed to have the missing files sent over. Until they arrive, the combined history carries a gap in that unit and no firm total. Deborah Jimenez asked that the gap be marked plainly in any interim material so it is not mistaken for a complete figure.

Sandra Flores asked for an early read on the size of the savings the review might find. Brenda Clayton declined to give one. The figure depends on the criteria work, which is not finished, and any number offered now would not hold. The group agreed to revisit the question once that work is drafted, rather than attach a figure the analysis cannot yet support.
Action items

rows: Send the missing unit's capital records
Complete the current-state process map, including the post-investment review step
Reconcile the spending history once the missing records arrive
Confirm the committee thresholds in force in each unit
Set the next working session once the criteria assessment is drafted

blocks.text: Status Report: Capital Allocation Review for Poole, Mcknight and Rush

Prepared by Brenda Clayton, with Deborah Jimenez. This report covers the period since the last update and sets out where the review for {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}} stands at its midpoint: what is finished, what comes next, and the two matters that could still move the outcome.
Where the work stands

We are about halfway through the engagement and on schedule. The current-state work is complete. We have documented how capital moves through the business, from the point a request is raised to the point it is funded, and we have assembled where the capital has actually gone over recent years. With that in hand we have started the assessment of how the client decides between competing requests. That assessment is the analytical core of the review and the piece the recommendation will rest on, and it is where most of the remaining effort will go. The work is being delivered within the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-003.fee}}, and nothing in the picture so far puts that at risk.
Done this period

items: Completed the map of the capital process, from how a request is raised through funding and, where it happens, the review that follows once the money is spent. The last step turned out to be the weakest documented, and we have said so plainly rather than smooth it over.

Reconciled the spending history across all units, including the one whose records arrived late. The combined view is now whole and carries a firm total rather than a qualified one, so the analysis that follows rests on a complete base.

Documented the criteria in use across the business and where they diverge. The divergence is real and material: the same request would clear in one unit and stall in another, because the units do not weigh requests the same way or against the same thresholds.
Mapped the approval thresholds unit by unit, so the governance recommendation will rest on how decisions are actually made rather than on how the policy reads on paper.
Confirmed the units in scope with the client, so the review covers the business the engagement letter described and does not drift into parts of it we were not asked to examine.

blocks.text: The period ahead

items: Design the method for ranking capital requests, so that one standard applies across the business rather than a different one in each unit. The method has to be plain enough that the client's own staff can apply it once we are gone.

Draft the governance process: who decides, at what threshold, on what record, and how a decision is revisited once the capital is committed. The weak post-investment step we found is part of what this has to correct.
Bring the first recommendations to the next working session, labeled as recommendations and kept separate from the findings that stand behind them, so that neither is mistaken for the other.

blocks.text: Risks

Two risks are worth naming, and both are being managed. The first was data: one unit's records arrived late and held up the spending history. They are now in, the history is reconciled, and that risk is closed. The second is more substantial. The criteria vary so widely across the business that a single ranking method will change how some units have long worked, and a method the client will not adopt is of no use. Deborah Jimenez is confirming where the real decision rights sit, so that the recommendation fits the client's actual authority rather than an idealized version of it. On cost there is no risk to report: the work remains within the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-003.fee}}, and the remaining effort is scoped against it.
The next report will accompany the first recommendations. If anything in this one warrants discussion before then, Deborah Jimenez or I will make the time for it.
Minutes of the working session on the Capital Allocation Review for {{fact:f:E-2019-003.client}}, recorded by Deborah Jimenez.
Attendees

items: Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager
Brenda Clayton, Consultant
Sandra Flores, Chief Operating Officer, Poole, Mcknight and Rush

blocks.text: Matters covered

The session reviewed the draft recommendations. The engagement is in its final stretch, and the purpose was to test the proposals against how the client actually operates before we commit them to the final material. Brenda Clayton took the group through the draft in the order the client would use it, from how a request enters the process to how a funded project is reviewed afterward.

Brenda Clayton presented the proposed method for ranking capital requests: a single hurdle rate applied across the business, a short strategic screen for fit, and a common request template so that every proposal reaches the committee in the same form. Sandra Flores agreed with the direction. She raised one concern, that a single hurdle rate may sit too high for maintenance spending that keeps existing operations running, and asked whether that category should be handled separately. Brenda Clayton agreed to carve out a distinct route for maintenance and compliance spending below a set size, so that necessary upkeep is not measured against the same bar as discretionary growth.

On governance, the draft proposes a standing capital committee, a fixed threshold above which a request must go to it, and a post-investment review on a set schedule. Sandra Flores questioned the threshold as drafted; at the level proposed the committee would meet on too many small items and lose the attention the large ones need. The group agreed to raise the threshold and to let units approve smaller requests within a delegated limit, with the committee reserved for the decisions that move the number. The post-investment review, which the current-state work found was done rarely and without a standard record, will be put on a fixed schedule with a short standard form.

The group also discussed how the new process would be introduced. Sandra Flores noted that a template and a committee only work if the units actually use them, and asked that the final recommendation say plainly how the change would be rolled out and who would own it inside the business. Brenda Clayton agreed to add a short section on adoption, covering the first cycle under the new process and who confirms that the units are following it.
Two points remain open before the final deliverable, and both were assigned below. Nothing else in the draft drew objection, and the shape of the recommendation is settled.
Follow-ups

rows: Add a separate route for maintenance and compliance spending below the set size
Set the committee threshold and the delegated approval limit for units
Confirm the committee membership and how often it meets
Fold the agreed changes into the final recommendation
Circulate the final draft to the client ahead of the closing review
